terms.definition: Streaming technique that detects viewer bandwidth in real time and switches between pre-encoded bitrate levels to maintain continuous playback.
Restricts who can view streams or assets via signed JWTs, API keys, or webhook authorization callbacks.
AES in Cipher Block Chaining mode — symmetric encryption where each plaintext block is XOR'd with the previous ciphertext block before encryption.
Secret unique identifier sent with API requests to authenticate the caller and authorize access to platform resources.
Stored video file (VOD) managed by Livepeer Studio, identified by a unique ID with associated metadata and playback URLs.
Authenticated Transfer Protocol — open decentralized social networking standard developed by Bluesky, enabling federated identity and data portability.
Graphical representation of a user or AI entity, ranging from 2D images to fully animated 3D digital characters driven by AI models.
Bidirectional predicted video frames that reference both preceding and following frames to achieve the highest compression ratio in a coded video stream.
Access token carried in an HTTP Authorization header, used by API clients to authenticate requests without re-sending credentials.
Number of bits conveyed per second of video; determines the data throughput rate of an encoded stream, directly affecting quality and file size.
Legacy term for a node that published streams and submitted video for transcoding; replaced by the term 'Gateway' in current protocol documentation.
Coalition for Content Provenance and Authenticity — open standard producing tamper-evident manifests that record the origin and edit history of media files.
Video encoding mode where the output data rate remains constant regardless of content complexity, trading compression efficiency for predictable file sizes.
Short excerpt from a livestream or VOD asset defined by start and end timestamps, used for highlights or shareable segments.
HTTP mechanism that lets servers specify which origins outside their own domain are allowed to make browser requests to their resources.
Encoding quality control parameter that targets consistent perceptual quality by adjusting quantization per frame; scale runs 0–51 with lower values producing higher quality.
Web-based management interface in Livepeer Studio for creating and managing streams, assets, API keys, and viewing analytics.
Livepeer's hosted real-time AI video platform that turns live camera input into AI-transformed visuals with sub-second latency.
Special Purpose Entity bringing embodied avatar workloads (Live2D, Three.js, Unreal Engine) into Livepeer as intelligent public pipelines.
Media file protected by encryption at rest so that only authorized parties holding the correct decryption key can access its content.
Specific URL path at which an API receives requests and returns responses for a defined operation.
Federation of social networking platforms that communicate via open protocols such as ActivityPub, enabling cross-platform interaction without centralized control.
Product by the MistServer team bridging Livepeer's transcoding infrastructure and real-world applications; provides libraries and integration tools for embedding Livepeer services.
Node that submits jobs, routes video or AI work to orchestrators, manages payment flows, and provides a protocol interface between users and the Livepeer Network.
Apple's adaptive streaming protocol that encodes video into multiple quality levels, segments them, and serves them with an index playlist (.m3u8) over standard HTTP.
Process of receiving a live video stream from a broadcaster's encoder into a media server, typically over RTMP, SRT, or WebRTC/WHIP.
Lightweight, human-readable data interchange format using key-value pairs and ordered lists, widely used for API request and response bodies.
Compact, URL-safe token format carrying signed claims used for stateless authentication; in video access control, a signed JWT proves viewer entitlement without a server round-trip for every request.
Distance in frames or seconds between consecutive keyframes (I-frames); a shorter interval improves seeking accuracy while a longer interval improves compression efficiency.
Time delay accumulating between video capture at the source and display on the viewer's device, incurred at every stage of the encode-ingest-transcode-deliver pipeline.
The live operational decentralized system of orchestrators, workers, gateways, and broadcasters performing video transcoding and AI inference work.
Hosted developer platform providing APIs, SDKs, and a dashboard for adding live and on-demand video experiences to applications, backed by the Livepeer Network.
Real-time or near-real-time transmission of video and audio over a network to viewers as it is captured, without pre-recording.
Open-source media server providing live video ingest, transcoding, and delivery capabilities, used within Livepeer's infrastructure to handle protocol translation and stream routing.
MPEG-4 Part 14 digital multimedia container format for storing video, audio, subtitles, and still images in a single file.
Simultaneous restreaming of a single live input to multiple external destination platforms (e.g., YouTube, Twitch) in a single broadcast session.
Free, open-source application for screen capture and live streaming, supporting RTMP, RTMPS, SRT, and WebRTC output protocols.
Supply-side operator contributing GPU or CPU resources to the Livepeer Network; receives jobs from gateways, performs transcoding or AI inference, and earns ETH fees and LPT rewards.
Public identifier for retrieving playback URLs for a stream or asset without exposing the private stream key or internal asset ID.
Access rules (public or JWT-required) attached to a stream or asset that determine what authentication viewers must present before playback is allowed.
Livepeer's embeddable video player component (lvpr.tv) with built-in support for HLS adaptive bitrate streaming and WebRTC low-latency fallback.
Organizational container in Livepeer Studio that groups related streams, assets, and API keys under a single namespace for multi-tenant or multi-environment management.
Verified chain of custody and edit history of a digital asset, confirming its origin and tracking modifications over time.
Rebuffering duration divided by total playback duration, expressing the fraction of viewing time spent waiting for the player to buffer data.
Stored archive of a live stream session automatically saved as a VOD asset when recording is enabled on the stream object.
Single encoded version of a source video at a specific resolution, bitrate, and codec configuration, produced during transcoding.
Pixel dimensions of a video frame expressed as width × height (e.g., 1920×1080); common tiers are 360p, 480p, 720p, 1080p, and 4K.
Architectural style for distributed hypermedia systems using standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for stateless resource interaction.
Multi-participant WebRTC video session managed by Livepeer Studio, enabling multiple users to simultaneously broadcast and receive audio and video.
TCP-based protocol for streaming audio, video, and data over a network, operating on port 1935; the dominant ingest protocol for live broadcasting software.
RTMP transported over a TLS/SSL connection, adding encryption to protect live video streams and metadata during ingest.
Collection of tools, libraries, and documentation enabling developers to build applications that integrate with a platform's APIs.
Time-sliced chunk of a video stream (typically 2–10 seconds) independently addressable over HTTP and used as the unit of transcoding and adaptive delivery.
Process of dividing a continuous video stream into short discrete chunks for HTTP-based delivery and adaptive bitrate switching between quality levels.
Active connection between a gateway and orchestrator, or in Studio terms, a single continuous broadcast period on a Stream object with its own metrics, recording, and viewership data.
Public/private cryptographic keypair used to sign and verify JWTs that gate access to access-controlled streams and assets in Livepeer Studio.
Treasury-funded organizational unit with a defined scope, budget, accountability structure, and term length, used to execute specific ecosystem workstreams.
Open-source UDP-based streaming protocol with packet recovery, low latency, and built-in AES encryption, designed for reliable transmission over unpredictable networks.
Top-level Livepeer Studio object representing a live broadcast channel, configured with a stream key, playback ID, transcoding profiles, and optional recording and multistream settings.
Secret credential used by broadcasters to authenticate and push live video to a stream's ingest endpoint; equivalent to a password for the RTMP or SRT connection.
Optimized real-time diffusion pipeline using stream batching and stochastic similarity filtering to apply generative image transformations to live video at interactive frame rates.
Project building the video infrastructure layer for decentralized social platforms, focused on the AT Protocol ecosystem and enabling open video publishing.
Reduced-size preview image representing a video frame, used for recognition, navigation, and social sharing previews.
Direct digital-to-digital conversion of video from one encoding configuration to another, producing multiple adaptive renditions at different resolutions and bitrates for cross-device delivery.
Duration from the moment a viewer presses play to the first video frame rendered on screen; a key quality-of-experience metric for streaming performance.
Resumable file upload protocol over HTTP that allows interrupted large file uploads to resume from where they stopped rather than restarting from the beginning.
Audience metrics including view counts, watch time, unique viewers, and geographic distribution tracked for streams and assets.
Video delivery model allowing users to access pre-recorded content at any time of their choosing, as opposed to a scheduled live broadcast.
HTTP callback mechanism where a server sends an automated POST request to a configured URL when a specified platform event occurs.
Open-source project and W3C/IETF standard providing browsers and mobile apps with peer-to-peer real-time audio, video, and data exchange over UDP.
W3C standard format for displaying timed text (captions, subtitles, chapters, metadata) synchronized with HTML5 video playback.
IETF draft protocol enabling viewers to watch content from streaming services via WebRTC using a standardized SDP offer/answer HTTP exchange.
RFC 9725 standard protocol for WebRTC-based live video ingestion via a simple HTTP SDP offer/answer exchange, enabling browser-native broadcasting without plugins.
Neural network that represents and predicts environment dynamics, enabling an AI agent to plan by simulating outcomes rather than acting purely from direct observation.
